PROJECT CODEX

Implementation Specification

Phased buildout from CLI tool to full coordination architecture

Engineering specs for public development and AI-assisted code generation

Nexum Corpus

Final Specification — March 2026

March 2026

## Table of Contents

## Overview

This document specifies the phased implementation of Project Codex. Each phase produces a publicly
shippable open-source artifact and is spec-complete for AI-assisted code generation. Claude Code
generates against these interfaces. Codex executes. Claude Code reviews.

### Workspace Structure

Cargo.toml (workspace)

```rust
[workspace]
members = [
  "crates/codex-core",      # Shared types, errors, config
  "crates/codex-parse",     # Tree-sitter + rowan CST bridge
  "crates/codex-graph",     # petgraph semantic code graph
  "crates/codex-coord",     # Intent, locking, CRDT coordination
  "crates/codex-validate",  # Type checking, constraints
  "crates/codex-eventlog",  # NATS JetStream event sourcing
  "crates/codex-lsp",       # tower-lsp proxy
  "crates/codex-cli",       # CLI binary
]
resolver = "2"

[workspace.dependencies]
tree-sitter = "0.26"
tree-sitter-language = "0.1"
tree-sitter-typescript = "0.23"
tree-sitter-python = "0.25"
tree-sitter-rust = "0.23"
rowan = "0.16"
petgraph = "0.8"
loro = "1"
async-nats = "0.46"
tower-lsp = "0.20"            # Monitor: consider tower-lsp-server fork
clap = { version = "4.5", features = ["derive"] }
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"
git2 = "0.20"
blake3 = "1"
thiserror = "2"
tracing = "0.1"
```

## Phase 0: Semantic Diff

The wedge product. A Git-compatible CLI that shows what changed at the function/class level, not the
line level. Immediate value, zero configuration, single static binary.

### What Ships

```
codex diff <ref-a> <ref-b> takes two Git refs and produces: which functions were added, removed, modified, moved, or had their signatures changed. Instead of “+47/-12 lines in auth.ts,” the output reads “Function validateToken: signature changed (added parameter refreshWindow: number).”
```

### Crates Activated

- codex-core — SemanticUnit, SemanticId, DiffKind, FileIdentity types
- codex-parse — Tree-sitter parsing, rowan CST bridge, semantic unit extraction
- codex-graph — petgraph DiGraph<SemanticNode, DependencyEdge>, graph diff
- codex-cli — CLI entry point with clap

### Core Types

codex-core/src/semantic.rs

```
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub struct SemanticUnit {
    pub id: SemanticId,             // Content-addressed hash
    pub kind: UnitKind,             // Function | Class | Interface | Module
    pub name: String,               // e.g., "validateToken"
    pub qualified_name: String,     // e.g., "auth::AuthManager::validateToken"
    pub file_path: PathBuf,
    pub byte_range: Range<usize>,
    pub signature_hash: u64,        // Hash of params + return type
    pub body_hash: u64,             // Hash of normalized body AST
    pub dependencies: Vec<SemanticId>,
}

pub type SemanticId = [u8; 32]; // BLAKE3 (256-bit)

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum UnitKind { Function, Method, Class, Struct, Interface, Trait, Enum, Module, Constant }

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct SemanticDiff {
    pub added: Vec<SemanticUnit>,
    pub removed: Vec<SemanticUnit>,
    pub modified: Vec<ModifiedUnit>,
    pub moved: Vec<MovedUnit>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct ModifiedUnit {
    pub before: SemanticUnit,
    pub after: SemanticUnit,
    pub changes: Vec<ChangeKind>, // SignatureChanged | BodyChanged | DocChanged
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct MovedUnit {
    pub unit: SemanticUnit,
    pub old_path: PathBuf,
    pub new_path: PathBuf,
}
```

codex-parse/src/lib.rs (trait)

```
/// Language-agnostic extraction interface.
/// Each language implements this with tree-sitter queries.
pub trait SemanticExtractor: Send + Sync {
    /// Supported file extensions
    fn extensions(&self) -> &[&str];

    /// Parse source bytes, return extracted semantic units
    fn extract(&self, path: &Path, source: &[u8]) -> Result<Vec<SemanticUnit>>;

    /// Extract dependency edges between units
    fn dependencies(&self, units: &[SemanticUnit], source: &[u8])
        -> Result<Vec<(SemanticId, SemanticId, DepKind)>>;
}

pub enum DepKind { Calls, Imports, Inherits, Implements, Uses }
```

codex-graph/src/lib.rs (core API)

```rust
pub struct CodeGraph {
    graph: DiGraph<SemanticNode, DepEdge>,
    index: HashMap<SemanticId, NodeIndex>,
}

impl CodeGraph {
    pub fn new() -> Self;
    pub fn add_unit(&mut self, unit: SemanticUnit) -> NodeIndex;
    pub fn add_dep(&mut self, from: SemanticId, to: SemanticId, kind: DepKind);
    pub fn get(&self, id: &SemanticId) -> Option<&SemanticUnit>;
    pub fn callers_of(&self, id: &SemanticId) -> Vec<&SemanticUnit>;
    pub fn deps_of(&self, id: &SemanticId) -> Vec<&SemanticUnit>;
    pub fn diff(&self, other: &CodeGraph) -> SemanticDiff;
}
```

### Pipeline

- Step 1: Git read. Use git2 to read file trees at both refs without checkout. Collect source files by extension.
- Step 2: Parse. Run appropriate SemanticExtractor per language. Tree-sitter queries extract function/class/interface nodes. Compute signature_hash and body_hash via BLAKE3 over normalized AST bytes.
- Step 3: Build CodeGraphs. One per ref. Nodes = SemanticUnits, edges = dependency relationships.
- Step 4: Diff. Match by qualified_name. Same name + different signature_hash = SignatureChanged. Same signature + different body_hash = BodyChanged. Same body_hash + different path = Moved. Unmatched = Added/Removed.
- Step 5: Output. --format json|text|github. JSON schema versioned (v1). GitHub format emits annotations for Actions.

### Language Support

Language

Grammar Crate

Extract Targets

Priority

TypeScript

tree-sitter-typescript

function_declaration, class_declaration, method_definition, interface_declaration, arrow_function
(named)

P0

Python

tree-sitter-python

function_definition, class_definition, decorated_definition

P0

Rust

tree-sitter-rust

function_item, impl_item, struct_item, enum_item, trait_item, mod_item

P1

Go

tree-sitter-go

function_declaration, method_declaration, type_declaration

P1

Java

tree-sitter-java

method_declaration, class_declaration, interface_declaration

P2

### Critical: Tree-sitter → Rowan Bridge

FIRST-OF-ITS-KIND

No tree-sitter → rowan bridge exists anywhere in the Rust ecosystem. rust-analyzer, Biome, Zed,
Helix, and Neovim all chose one or the other—never both. This bridge is the first novel engineering
contribution of Phase 0 and the foundation every subsequent phase depends on.

Tree-sitter provides battle-tested incremental parsing for 200+ languages. Rowan provides lossless,
refactoring-friendly structural editing. Combining them yields multi-language incremental parsing
with lossless CST manipulation—a capability no existing tool provides.

#### Bridge Algorithm

codex-parse/src/bridge.rs (core algorithm)

```
/// Walk a tree-sitter CST and drive rowan's GreenNodeBuilder.
/// Synthesizes WHITESPACE tokens for byte-range gaps between
/// tree-sitter nodes (tree-sitter makes whitespace implicit).
fn bridge_node(
    ts_node: tree_sitter::Node,
    source: &[u8],
    builder: &mut GreenNodeBuilder,
    kind_map: &KindMap,
) {
    let rowan_kind = kind_map.to_rowan(ts_node.kind_id(), ts_node.is_named());
    builder.start_node(rowan_kind);

    let mut last_end = ts_node.start_byte();
    let child_count = ts_node.child_count();

    for i in 0..child_count {
        let child = ts_node.child(i).unwrap();

        // Synthesize whitespace/trivia for gaps
        if child.start_byte() > last_end {
            let trivia = &source[last_end..child.start_byte()];
            emit_trivia(trivia, builder, kind_map);
        }

        if child.child_count() == 0 {
            // Leaf: emit as token
            let text = &source[child.start_byte()..child.end_byte()];
            let kind = kind_map.to_rowan(child.kind_id(), child.is_named());
            builder.token(kind, std::str::from_utf8(text).unwrap());
        } else {
            // Branch: recurse
            bridge_node(child, source, builder, kind_map);
        }
        last_end = child.end_byte();
    }

    // Trailing trivia within this node
    if last_end < ts_node.end_byte() {
        let trivia = &source[last_end..ts_node.end_byte()];
        emit_trivia(trivia, builder, kind_map);
    }
    builder.finish_node();
}

/// Emit trivia bytes, splitting into WHITESPACE, COMMENT, NEWLINE.
fn emit_trivia(bytes: &[u8], builder: &mut GreenNodeBuilder, km: &KindMap);

/// Losslessness invariant (debug assertion):
/// Concatenating all rowan tokens must exactly reproduce source.
fn assert_lossless(green: &GreenNode, source: &[u8]);
```

#### Five Engineering Challenges

- Whitespace gap recovery: Tree-sitter’s extras system makes whitespace implicit. The bridge must detect byte-range gaps between child nodes and synthesize WHITESPACE/NEWLINE/COMMENT tokens. Requires access to original source bytes.
- SyntaxKind mapping: Tree-sitter uses string node kinds; rowan uses SyntaxKind(u16). Auto-generate the KindMap from each grammar’s node-types.json (shipped as NODE_TYPES: &str in every grammar crate).
- Named vs. anonymous nodes: Tree-sitter distinguishes named nodes (grammar rules) from anonymous nodes (punctuation like +, ;, {). Both need SyntaxKind values in rowan.
- Error and extra nodes: is_error(), is_missing(), is_extra() nodes need corresponding rowan kinds and must be handled without breaking losslessness.
- Incremental re-bridging: When tree-sitter incrementally re-parses after an edit, only the changed subtrees should be re-bridged into rowan. Use Tree::changed_ranges() to identify invalidated regions.

#### Tree-sitter 0.24+ Migration Note

```rust
Tree-sitter 0.24 fundamentally changed grammar loading. Grammar crates now export a LANGUAGE: LanguageFn constant instead of a fn language() -> Language function. The new pattern requires the tree-sitter-language crate:
```

Grammar loading (0.26+)

```
// CORRECT for tree-sitter 0.26+
use tree_sitter_language::LanguageFn;

parser.set_language(
    &tree_sitter_typescript::LANGUAGE_TYPESCRIPT.into()
).unwrap();
// Note: TypeScript crate exports LANGUAGE_TYPESCRIPT and LANGUAGE_TSX
```

### Acceptance Criteria

- codex diff HEAD~1 HEAD on a 100K LOC TypeScript repo completes in <2 seconds.
- Moved functions detected as moves, not add+delete.
- Signature changes distinguished from body-only changes.
- JSON output stable and schema-versioned.
- Zero configuration. Language detection by extension. Single static binary.

### Public Deliverable

```
Open-source Rust binary. Apache 2.0. Published to crates.io. Homebrew tap. Prebuilt binaries for Linux/macOS/Windows. README with demo GIF: semantic diff vs. git diff side by side.
```

## Phase 1: Semantic Conflict Detection

```
Pre-merge conflict detection at the semantic level. The thing that saves teams money today. Git merge succeeds textually; codex check catches the semantic conflicts that break at runtime.
```

### What Ships

```
codex check <branch-a> <branch-b> identifies semantic conflicts that text-based merge misses: Agent A renames a function, Agent B calls the old name. Agent A changes a return type, Agent B depends on it. Agent A deletes a class, Agent B extends it.
```

### New Crate

- codex-coord (partial) — ConflictDetector engine. Three-way semantic diff (base → A, base → B) with cross-reference analysis.

### Core Types

codex-coord/src/conflict.rs

```
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct SemanticConflict {
    pub kind: ConflictKind,
    pub severity: Severity,  // Error | Warning | Info
    pub unit_a: SemanticUnit,
    pub unit_b: SemanticUnit,
    pub description: String,
    pub suggestion: Option<String>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum ConflictKind {
    BrokenReference { caller: SemanticId, callee: SemanticId },
    ConcurrentBodyEdit { unit: SemanticId },
    SignatureMismatch { function: SemanticId, caller: SemanticId },
    DeletedDependency { deleted: SemanticId, dependent: SemanticId },
    NamingCollision { name: String },
    InterfaceDrift { interface_id: SemanticId, implementor: SemanticId },
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum Severity { Error, Warning, Info }
```

codex-coord/src/detector.rs (trait)

```rust
pub struct ConflictDetector;

impl ConflictDetector {
    /// Detect semantic conflicts between two branches.
    /// Computes merge_base internally via git2.
    pub fn detect(
        repo: &git2::Repository,
        branch_a: &str,
        branch_b: &str,
    ) -> Result<Vec<SemanticConflict>> {
        let base = find_merge_base(repo, branch_a, branch_b)?;
        let graph_base = build_graph(repo, &base)?;
        let graph_a = build_graph(repo, branch_a)?;
        let graph_b = build_graph(repo, branch_b)?;
        let diff_a = graph_base.diff(&graph_a);
        let diff_b = graph_base.diff(&graph_b);
        cross_reference(diff_a, diff_b, &graph_a, &graph_b)
    }
}

/// Cross-reference: for each unit deleted/renamed/signature-changed
/// in diff_a, check if any unit in graph_b depends on the old version.
fn cross_reference(
    diff_a: SemanticDiff, diff_b: SemanticDiff,
    graph_a: &CodeGraph, graph_b: &CodeGraph,
) -> Result<Vec<SemanticConflict>>;
```

### Pipeline

- Step 1: Find merge base via git2::Repository::merge_base().
- Step 2: Build three CodeGraphs: base, branch_a, branch_b.
- Step 3: Compute SemanticDiff base→A and base→B.
- Step 4: For each removed/renamed/signature-changed unit in A, search B’s graph for callers/dependents of the old version. Vice versa.
- Step 5: For units modified in both diffs, classify: independent (different units = OK), additive (both add methods to same class = OK with warning), conflicting (both modify same function body = Error).
- Step 6: Output report. Exit 0 = clean, 1 = errors found, 2 = warnings only.

### Example Output

```
$ codex check feature/oauth feature/billing
Semantic Conflicts: 2 errors, 1 warning

  ERROR  BrokenReference
         Branch: feature/billing
         processPayment() calls validateToken()
         But feature/oauth renamed validateToken -> verifyToken
         Suggestion: Update call site to verifyToken()

  ERROR  SignatureMismatch
         Branch: feature/oauth  
         refreshSession() now returns Result<Token, AuthError>
         But feature/billing calls refreshSession() expecting Token
         Suggestion: Handle Result wrapper in billing code

  WARN   ConcurrentBodyEdit
         Both branches modify AuthManager.logout()
         feature/oauth: added token invalidation
         feature/billing: added billing cleanup
         Suggestion: Manual review required
```

### Acceptance Criteria

- Detects BrokenReference, SignatureMismatch, DeletedDependency with zero false negatives on a curated 50-scenario test suite.
- False positive rate below 15%.
- Completes in <5s on 100K LOC repos with <1000 changed units across branches.
- GitHub Action: uses: nexum-corpus/codex-check@v1
- Pre-merge hook: codex check --install-hook

### Public Deliverable

```
New subcommand codex check. GitHub Action on marketplace. Blog post with real conflict examples from multi-agent repos.
```

## Phase 2: Coordination Server

Real-time intent declaration and semantic locking for concurrent agents. The first component that
requires a running process rather than a CLI invocation.

### What Ships

```
codex serve starts a lightweight coordination server. Agents register intent before writing code. The server validates intent against the live semantic graph, acquires locks at function/class granularity, and rejects conflicting intents before compute is wasted.
```

### New Crates Activated

- codex-coord (full) — IntentManager, SemanticLockTable, coordination protocol
- codex-validate (partial) — Pre-commit validation hooks

### Core Types

codex-coord/src/intent.rs

```
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct IntentPayload {
    pub id: Uuid,
    pub agent_id: String,
    pub timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub description: String,          // Human-readable
    pub target_units: Vec<SemanticId>, // Units agent plans to modify
    pub estimated_changes: Vec<PlannedChange>,
    pub ttl: Duration,                // Auto-expire if not committed
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum PlannedChange {
    ModifyBody { unit: SemanticId },
    ModifySignature { unit: SemanticId, new_params: Vec<String> },
    AddUnit { parent: SemanticId, kind: UnitKind, name: String },
    RemoveUnit { unit: SemanticId },
    MoveUnit { unit: SemanticId, new_parent: SemanticId },
    RenameUnit { unit: SemanticId, new_name: String },
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum IntentResult {
    Approved { lock_token: Uuid, expires: DateTime<Utc> },
    Queued { position: usize, estimated_wait: Duration },
    Rejected { conflicts: Vec<IntentConflict> },
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct IntentConflict {
    pub blocking_intent: Uuid,
    pub blocking_agent: String,
    pub contested_unit: SemanticId,
    pub reason: String,
}
```

codex-coord/src/lock.rs

```rust
pub struct SemanticLockTable {
    locks: DashMap<SemanticId, LockEntry>,
}

pub struct LockEntry {
    pub holder: String,       // agent_id
    pub intent_id: Uuid,
    pub lock_kind: LockKind,
    pub acquired: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub expires: DateTime<Utc>,
}

pub enum LockKind {
    Exclusive,  // ModifyBody, ModifySignature, Remove, Rename
    Shared,     // Read-only analysis, AddUnit (to different parent)
}

impl SemanticLockTable {
    pub fn acquire(&self, unit: SemanticId, kind: LockKind, agent: &str, intent: Uuid) -> Result<LockEntry>;
    pub fn release(&self, unit: &SemanticId, token: &Uuid) -> Result<()>;
    pub fn check(&self, unit: &SemanticId) -> Option<&LockEntry>;
    pub fn expire_stale(&self);  // Called on timer
}
```

### Protocol

HTTP/JSON API (tiny footprint, easy for any agent to call):

Method

Endpoint

Request

Response

POST

/intent/declare

IntentPayload

IntentResult

POST

/intent/commit

{ intent_id, mutations }

CommitResult

POST

/intent/abort

{ intent_id }

Ok

GET

/graph/query

GraphQuery

Vec<SemanticUnit>

GET

/locks

(none)

Vec<LockEntry>

WS

/events

(subscribe)

Stream<CoordEvent>

### Agent Integration Pattern

Agent workflow (pseudocode)

```
// 1. Agent analyzes task, identifies target units
targets = codex_client.query_graph("callers_of", "validateToken")

// 2. Declare intent
result = codex_client.declare_intent({
    agent_id: "agent-oauth-7",
    description: "Refactoring validateToken to support OAuth2",
    target_units: [validateToken.id, AuthManager.id],
    planned_changes: [
        ModifySignature { unit: validateToken.id, new_params: ["token", "provider"] },
        ModifyBody { unit: validateToken.id },
    ],
    ttl: "15m",
})

// 3. If approved, generate code
if result.approved:
    lock_token = result.lock_token
    // ... generate code mutations ...
    codex_client.commit(intent_id, mutations, lock_token)
elif result.rejected:
    // Wait or pick different task
    log("Conflict with agent {}", result.conflicts[0].blocking_agent)
```

### Acceptance Criteria

- Two agents declaring overlapping intents: one approved, one rejected/queued, within <50ms.
- Non-overlapping intents both approved concurrently.
- Stale locks auto-expire after TTL.
- WebSocket event stream delivers intent/lock/commit events to all connected agents within <100ms.
- Server handles 100 concurrent agents with <10ms p99 intent declaration latency.
- Single binary, zero external dependencies in dev mode (embedded graph, no NATS required yet).

### Public Deliverable

```
codex serve command. Docker image. API docs. Client SDK crate (codex-client). Integration example with Claude Code showing declare→generate→commit workflow.
```

## Phase 3: Event Log + Semantic Rollback

Every approved mutation becomes an event in an append-only log. Enables: full audit trail, semantic
rollback (“undo the OAuth feature”), causal chain queries (“why was this function changed?”), and
state replay.

### What Ships

```
codex log shows the semantic event history. codex rollback <intent-id> generates compensating mutations that undo a specific intent’s changes without reverting unrelated work.
```

### New Crate

- codex-eventlog — NATS JetStream integration, event schema, replay engine, rollback generator

### Core Types

codex-eventlog/src/event.rs

```
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct SemanticEvent {
    pub id: Uuid,
    pub timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub intent_id: Uuid,
    pub agent_id: String,
    pub description: String,
    pub mutations: Vec<Mutation>,
    pub parent_event: Option<Uuid>,  // Causal chain
    pub tags: Vec<String>,           // e.g., ["feature:oauth", "sprint:12"]
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum Mutation {
    AddUnit { unit: SemanticUnit },
    RemoveUnit { id: SemanticId, snapshot: SemanticUnit },
    ModifyUnit { id: SemanticId, before: SemanticUnit, after: SemanticUnit },
    MoveUnit { id: SemanticId, from: PathBuf, to: PathBuf },
    RenameUnit { id: SemanticId, from: String, to: String },
}

impl Mutation {
    /// Generate the compensating mutation that undoes this one.
    pub fn compensate(&self) -> Mutation {
        match self {
            Mutation::AddUnit { unit } =>
                Mutation::RemoveUnit { id: unit.id, snapshot: unit.clone() },
            Mutation::RemoveUnit { id, snapshot } =>
                Mutation::AddUnit { unit: snapshot.clone() },
            Mutation::ModifyUnit { id, before, after } =>
                Mutation::ModifyUnit { id: *id, before: after.clone(), after: before.clone() },
            Mutation::MoveUnit { id, from, to } =>
                Mutation::MoveUnit { id: *id, from: to.clone(), to: from.clone() },
            Mutation::RenameUnit { id, from, to } =>
                Mutation::RenameUnit { id: *id, from: to.clone(), to: from.clone() },
        }
    }
}
```

### Rollback Algorithm

- Step 1: Find all events with matching intent_id, ordered by timestamp.
- Step 2: For each mutation in reverse order, generate compensating mutation via .compensate().
- Step 3: Validate compensating mutations against current graph state (the target unit may have been further modified by later intents).
- Step 4: If validation passes, apply and emit a new SemanticEvent with tag “rollback:{original_intent_id}”. If conflicts exist, report them and require manual resolution.

### Acceptance Criteria

- Full audit trail: every mutation traceable to agent + intent + timestamp.
- codex rollback <intent-id> cleanly reverses a feature addition when no subsequent intents touched the same units.
- Conflicting rollback (unit modified by later intent) detected and reported rather than silently applied.
- State replay: codex replay --to <event-id> rebuilds the semantic graph at any historical point.
- NATS JetStream persistence: events survive server restart.

### Public Deliverable

```
codex log and codex rollback commands. NATS JetStream integration (optional — falls back to local SQLite event store for dev mode). Blog post demonstrating semantic rollback of an AI-generated feature.
```

## Phase 4: IDE Integration (LSP Shim)

The compatibility layer that makes the entire system invisible to existing toolchains. Human
developers use VS Code / Cursor / Neovim normally. Agent activity appears as inline diagnostics. The
semantic graph is queryable via custom LSP methods.

### What Ships

codex-lsp binary that acts as an LSP proxy between the editor and the underlying language server
(tsserver, pyright, rust-analyzer). It intercepts textDocument/didChange events, translates them
into semantic mutations, and injects Codex-specific diagnostics and code lenses.

### New Crate

- codex-lsp — tower-lsp server + proxy layer

#### Dependency Warning: tower-lsp

MIGRATION RISK

tower-lsp (ebkalderon, v0.20) is slowing in maintenance. Its lsp-types dependency became
unmaintained. Two alternatives exist: tower-lsp-server (community fork, active, drops #[async_trait]
for native async fn, uses ls-types fork) and async-lsp (Tower Layer architecture, better suited for
proxy patterns). Recommend starting with tower-lsp 0.20 for its 63-method LanguageServer trait and
large ecosystem, with an explicit migration path to tower-lsp-server or async-lsp if the original
stalls.

### Custom LSP Methods

Method

Direction

Purpose

codex/activeLocks

Server → Client

Show which functions are locked by which agents as code lenses

codex/agentIntent

Server → Client

Display pending agent intents as inline annotations

codex/semanticDiff

Client → Server

Request semantic diff for current file vs. branch

codex/validationStatus

Server → Client

Push real-time validation results as diagnostics

codex/eventStream

Server → Client

Stream semantic events (agent commits, rollbacks) to editor

### Architecture

Editor ↔ codex-lsp ↔ upstream LSP (tsserver, pyright, etc.)

The proxy passes through all standard LSP messages to the upstream server unchanged. It intercepts
textDocument/didChange to also update the Codex semantic graph, and merges its own diagnostics with
the upstream server’s diagnostics. Custom methods are namespaced under codex/ and ignored by editors
that don’t support them.

### Acceptance Criteria

- VS Code extension that auto-starts codex-lsp and configures it as a proxy.
- Code lenses showing “Agent oauth-7 is editing this function” on locked units.
- Inline diagnostics from validation layer appear within 200ms of save.
- Standard LSP functionality (completions, go-to-def, hover) unaffected — zero latency overhead on pass-through.
- Graceful degradation: if codex serve is not running, editor functions normally with no errors.

### Public Deliverable

VS Code extension on marketplace. Neovim configuration guide. codex-lsp binary. Demo video showing:
agent declares intent → code lens appears in IDE → agent commits → validation runs → diagnostics
update → human reviews in IDE.

## Appendix: Claude Code Generation Protocol

This section defines how Claude Code and Codex interact with these specs during the build.

### Workflow

- Step 1: Claude Code reads the phase spec for the current phase.
- Step 2: Claude Code generates implementation code for each crate, targeting the trait interfaces and type definitions specified above.
- Step 3: Claude Code writes tests against the acceptance criteria.
- Step 4: Codex executes the implementation tasks: filling in function bodies, writing tree-sitter query files, implementing the graph diff algorithm, etc.
- Step 5: Claude Code reviews Codex’s output against the spec: do types match? Do tests pass? Are acceptance criteria met?
- Step 6: Iterate until cargo test passes and cargo clippy is clean.

### Spec Authority Rules

- Types are authoritative. The struct/enum definitions in this spec are the source of truth. Codex must not alter type signatures without explicit approval.
- Trait interfaces are contracts. The function signatures on traits are the API boundary. Implementation details are Codex’s domain.
- Acceptance criteria are tests. Each criterion maps to one or more integration tests. Claude Code generates the test; Codex writes code that passes it.
- Pipeline steps are ordered. The numbered steps in each phase define execution order. Codex implements each step as a distinct function or module.

### Quality Gates

- cargo build --release compiles without warnings.
- cargo test passes all unit and integration tests.
- cargo clippy -- -D warnings clean.
- cargo fmt --check passes.
- No unsafe blocks without documented justification.
- All public types have doc comments.
- Benchmark suite (criterion) for performance-critical paths (parsing, graph diff, conflict detection).

### Rowan Key API Reference

GreenNodeBuilder (rowan 0.16)

```rust
pub struct GreenNodeBuilder<'cache>;

impl GreenNodeBuilder<'_> {
    pub fn new() -> GreenNodeBuilder<'static>
    pub fn with_cache(cache: &mut NodeCache) -> GreenNodeBuilder<'_>
    pub fn token(&mut self, kind: SyntaxKind, text: &str)
    pub fn start_node(&mut self, kind: SyntaxKind)
    pub fn finish_node(&mut self)
    pub fn checkpoint(&self) -> Checkpoint
    pub fn start_node_at(&mut self, cp: Checkpoint, kind: SyntaxKind)
    pub fn finish(self) -> GreenNode
}

// Language trait (0.16 — requires Ord + Hash)
pub trait Language: Sized + Copy + Debug + Eq + Ord + Hash {
    type Kind: Sized + Copy + Debug + Eq + Ord + Hash;
    fn kind_from_raw(raw: SyntaxKind) -> Self::Kind;
    fn kind_to_raw(kind: Self::Kind) -> SyntaxKind;
}

// NOTE: GreenNode is Send + Sync (shareable).
// SyntaxNode is NOT Send/Sync (per-thread red facade).
```

### Loro Key API Reference

LoroTree (loro 1.x)

```
let doc = LoroDoc::new();
doc.set_peer_id(42).unwrap();
let tree = doc.get_tree("ast");
tree.enable_fractional_index(0);

// Create, move, delete
let root = tree.create(None).unwrap();
let child = tree.create(root).unwrap();
tree.mov(child, root).unwrap();
tree.mov_to(child, root, 0).unwrap();     // To specific index
tree.mov_after(child, sibling).unwrap();   // After sibling
tree.delete(child).unwrap();

// Metadata per node
let meta = tree.get_meta(root).unwrap();   // Returns LoroMap
meta.insert("kind", "function_def");

// Export/Import
let bytes = doc.export(ExportMode::all_updates()).unwrap();
doc.import(&bytes).unwrap();
let snapshot = doc.export(ExportMode::Snapshot).unwrap();

// Time travel
doc.checkout(&frontiers);
doc.checkout_to_latest();
let forked = doc.fork();
```

### Ecosystem Architecture Patterns

The workspace structure follows the Helix model (consistent prefix, explicit member list) with rust-
analyzer’s layering discipline (explicit API boundaries between parse → graph → coordination →
validation) and Zed’s dependency management pattern (workspace.dependencies with dep.workspace =
true per crate).

- rust-analyzer (~40 crates): Three API boundaries: syntax, hir, ide. Parser is tree-agnostic event stream. Uses ungrammar for CST shape definition.
- Helix (~11 crates): helix- prefix throughout. default-members points to binary crate only.
- Zed (~200 crates): Flat namespace under crates/*. Short lowercase names. publish = false workspace-wide.

BUILD PHILOSOPHY

Ship Phase 0 fast. It is the proof that this architecture produces real tools, not just whitepapers.
Every subsequent phase extends the same codebase, the same types, the same binary. By Phase 4, the
system is the full 5-layer architecture — but it was useful from day one.

— End of Implementation Specification —
